imageCaption: A coffee shop with five identical versions of the same table simultaneously existing, each one occupied by a different possible moment. Aaliyah stands in the center, holding her diagram notebook, looking at her own reflection across temporal layers.
text:
The problem with arriving in Madrid at 6:47 PM was that Aaliyah arrived in Madrid at 6:47 PM, 5:23 PM, 8:14 PM, and 3:09 PM simultaneously.

She was aware of all four versions of her arrival in a way that suggested consciousness could apparently multitask if the timeline decided to stop being linear about it. Four different sets of eyes were seeing four different versions of the same airport terminal. Four different versions of her brain were processing four different levels of disorientation.

This was, objectively, the worst glitch yet.

"Okay," she said, which came out as four overlapping versions of "okay" that harmonized in a way that probably violated some laws of sound engineering. "This is new."

Anthony appeared in three of the versions simultaneously. In the 6:47 PM version, he was holding a suitcase. In the 5:23 PM version, he hadn't picked up luggage yet. In the 3:09 PM version, he was eating a sandwich that didn't exist in the other timelines. The 8:14 PM version of her father was busy arguing with a security guard about whether a morpho butterfly required customs documentation.

"You're multiplied," he said from all three versions at once. "Congratulations. You have achieved temporal duplication."

"This is not what I wanted when I wished to be in two places at once," Aaliyah said.

"You wished to be in two places at once?" Luisana appeared, somehow existing in all four versions with perfect simultaneity, which suggested her mother had either mastered this or accepted it so completely that the universe had stopped bothering to make it weird for her. She looked exactly the same in every timeline—calm, slightly amused, taking detailed notes in her journal as though multiplying across time was a perfectly normal travel inconvenience. "When did you make that wish?"

"When I was ten," Aaliyah said. "Elijah's birthday party. You wanted me to simultaneously help with the cake and run interference on him destroying the living room with glitter."

"And the universe is apparently paying your childhood self back with interest," her mother said. She looked up from the journal in all four versions. "Okay. The glitch is spatial-temporal compression. You're not actually multiplied. You're superimposed. Every decision you made about how to move through that airport happened in a different moment, and the timeline is just choosing to show all of them at once."

"Superimposed," Aaliyah repeated. "So I'm not four versions of myself. I'm one version of myself spread across four moments and I'm experiencing all of them consciously which is—"

"Which is exactly how time works," her mother said gently, "we're just usually not aware of how linear our perception is until it stops being."

Elijah emerged from a timeline that definitely should not have contained Elijah—the 3:09 PM version, which was technically forty minutes before they landed. He was holding a Madrid street map that he'd somehow acquired from a future-version of the airport gift shop and eating the same sandwich that Anthony-at-8:14-PM was eating, which suggested either timeline coherence or a very committed commitment to food continuity.

"I found a thing," Elijah announced from that version only, which meant the glitch was starting to develop personalities. "Or I'm about to find it. Or I already found it. Time is weird."

"What thing?" all four versions of Aaliyah asked simultaneously.

"Come here," he said, gesturing to the 3:09 PM timeline. "But also don't, because you're already here in the other times and moving is going to make this worse."

Aaliyah found herself walking toward Elijah while simultaneously standing completely still, which was exactly as painful as she'd theoretically expected. The sensation of moving through temporal layers felt like swimming through moments, each one with its own gravity, its own air pressure, its own specific quality of existing.

When she reached the 3:09 PM timeline—or when that version of her reached that version of Elijah; the grammar was starting to hurt—her brother was pointing at a poster on the airport wall.

It was an advertisement for a tourism company. Specifically, an advertisement for Curaçao tourism. Specifically, an advertisement for a specific hotel in Willemstad with a very specific architectural feature: a yellow door.

The poster was in five versions too, each one at a slightly different distance from them, existing in all the times at once. In some versions it was crisp and new. In others it was faded. In one version, it was being put up by a construction worker who looked startled to see multiple versions of a teenager staring at him from across temporal layers.

"It's everywhere," Elijah said simply. "The thing we're supposed to find. Or the thing that's supposed to find us. It's already here, in every time, waiting for us to notice it."

Anthony—all three versions that existed in this timeline—gathered around the poster with the intensity of someone who'd just found the next license plate for his collection. "Yellow doors," he said thoughtfully. "That's not subtle."

"Subtlety died around Day 4," Luisana said. She was somehow holding steady in all four versions, her journal updating itself across temporal spaces, recording data in a language that was probably going to need its own translator. "The universe stopped bothering to be coy. Now it's just putting road signs in front of us and hoping we understand the language."

Sully appeared—not in all four timelines, which suggested he had some control over his temporal state that the rest of them didn't—and barked once, firmly. One long bark. Major glitch incoming.

"It's not incoming," Aaliyah said, feeling the shape of understanding click into place like a diagram organizing itself. "It's here. We're inside the glitch. We have been for the last three minutes, and we didn't notice because the glitch decided to let us experience it all at once instead of choosing one version of arrival."

"Why would it do that?" Anthony asked.

"Because we needed to see all the versions," Aaliyah said. "Every choice we could have made about how to walk through that terminal, every moment we could have been in, every possibility of arrival—the glitch is showing us that they're all happening. We're not singular. We're multivalent. We're every choice we could have made and every way we could have made it."

She looked at her mother across the temporal layers. Luisana was smiling—in all four versions of her face simultaneously.

"That's your diagram," her mother said. "That's what you've been drawing all this time. Not a map of glitches. A map of possibility. And the glitch is telling you that you've been doing it right."

The airport began to compress. The four timelines started folding back into each other, singular moments deciding to become linear again. But Aaliyah could feel something shifting. Not her understanding—that had changed. Something deeper. The way the universe saw her. The way it treated her choices as though they were all real, all valid, all leading somewhere.

They landed in 6:47 PM Madrid, singular and solid and present in only one moment.

But Aaliyah could still feel the echo of the other versions, like ghosts of choice still existing somewhere in the topology of time.

"Well," Anthony said, picking up their suitcase in the singular now, "that happened."

"That happened," Aaliyah agreed. She pulled out her diagram notebook and started drawing: five versions of the same airport terminal, each one a different moment, all of them converging toward a yellow door. "And somewhere in all of this, we're supposed to understand that getting to Curaçao isn't about choosing the right path. It's about walking all the paths at once and trusting that they lead the same place."

Elijah was still holding his future-purchased map of Madrid, which suggested that temporal glitches occasionally left you souvenirs.

"North," he said. "We should still go north."

Luisana was already moving toward the terminal exit, her journal full of notes that probably made more sense than anything Aaliyah could currently articulate.

"North," she agreed. "Toward the next glitch. Toward the yellow door. Toward whatever the universe is trying to teach us about being multiple versions of ourselves walking a single path."

Sully barked again—not a warning this time. A confirmation.

And somewhere over Madrid, a morpho butterfly that shouldn't have existed in this timeline at all caught the light and kept heading northeast.
